text:
Autumn wins, and the case is simple.

The colors are the obvious starting point — reds, oranges, yellows all present at once, not competing but working together. No other season produces that range simultaneously. Spring is one note. Summer is uniform green. Winter collapses into gray and white.

But color alone does not explain it. Autumn has texture too. Leaves on the ground, mist in the morning, low-angle light that makes shadows longer and softer. The quality of October light is genuinely different from summer light — cooler, more directional, better at defining surfaces.

There is also the contrast factor. Decay and beauty occurring together creates visual tension that the other seasons lack. A single red leaf against dark wet pavement does more work than an entire summer garden.

Winter has clarity. Spring has energy. Summer has abundance. But autumn is the only season doing several interesting things at once. That density of effect is what makes it the strongest aesthetic choice.
